text: WHO Model List of Essential Medicines: Reproductive Health Subset

The WHO Model List of Essential Medicines (EML) is a list of medications considered to be most effective and safe to meet health-care needs. This document presents the subset of the 23rd edition (2023) relevant to reproductive health, maternal care, contraception, and sexually transmitted infection management.

The essential medicines listed below are those that should be available in all primary health-care facilities, district hospitals, and tertiary referral centres. Availability of these medicines is critical for preventing maternal and neonatal mortality.
Maternal Health and Labour Management
Active Management of Third Stage of Labour and PPH Prevention

caption: Essential Medicines for Active Management of Third Stage of Labour
rows: PPH prevention in low-resource settings; easier to store than oxytocin

text: Oxytocin is the gold standard uterotonic for active management of the third stage of labour. However, oxytocin requires refrigeration in most formulations and IV administration capability. In low-resource settings without reliable cold chain, misoprostol (600 μg rectally) is an acceptable alternative for PPH prevention, though less effective than oxytocin.
Treatment of Severe Complications in Pregnancy and Labour

caption: Essential Medicines for Pregnancy Complications
rows: 10-20 mg PO, repeat 20 mg if needed
10 IU IM or 20-40 IU IV infusion

text: Magnesium sulfate is the anticonvulsant of choice for eclampsia and is superior to other agents. It must be available in all facilities providing obstetric care. Close monitoring for signs of toxicity (loss of reflexes, respiratory depression) is essential, particularly in settings with limited laboratory capabilities.
Antenatal Care Essentials
Iron and Folic Acid Supplementation

caption: Essential Micronutrient Supplements in Pregnancy
rows: All pregnant women (especially in areas with low dietary intake)

text: Universal iron supplementation in pregnancy prevents anaemia and reduces the risk of preterm birth and low birth weight. In endemic areas of malaria, iron supplementation should be coupled with intermittent preventive treatment (IPT) with antimalarials.
Tetanus and Immunization in Pregnancy

items: Tetanus toxoid: Safe in pregnancy; prevents neonatal tetanus. Give 2 doses if no prior immunization, or booster if >10 years since last dose.
Influenza vaccine: Recommended in pregnancy; safe and reduces risk of severe infection and adverse birth outcomes.
COVID-19 vaccine: Recommended in all trimesters; safe and effective in pregnancy.

text: Management of Reproductive Tract Infections
Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs) in Pregnancy

caption: Treatment of STIs During Pregnancy
rows: Amoxicillin 500 mg x 3 daily x 7 days
2 g PO single dose or 400 mg x 2 daily x 5 days
Avoid in first trimester if possible (use after 12 weeks); Category B
300 mg PO x 2 daily x 7 days (or 2% cream x 7 nights)
Metronidazole 400-500 mg x 2 daily x 5-7 days
None (both safe); but early treatment reduces risk of preterm birth

text: Untreated STIs in pregnancy are associated with increased risk of preterm birth, early rupture of membranes, intrauterine infection, and transmission to the neonate. All pregnant women should be screened for syphilis and HIV at the first antenatal visit. Chlamydia and gonorrhoea screening is recommended at first visit and repeated in third trimester.
Urinary Tract Infection and Asymptomatic Bacteriuria in Pregnancy

caption: Treatment of UTI and Asymptomatic Bacteriuria in Pregnancy
rows: Screen and treat all pregnant women; untreated ASB leads to pyelonephritis in 20-40%
Avoid at term (risk of haemolytic anaemia in G6PD); or use cephalexin
500 mg x 4 daily (or 1-2 g IM/IV daily)
Requires hospitalization; may progress to sepsis if untreated

text: Contraception and Family Planning
Essential Contraceptive Methods in Public Health Systems

caption: WHO-Recommended Contraceptive Methods for Public Health Facilities
rows: Highly effective; reversible; 10+ years duration; no hormonal effects
Very effective; lighter periods; lasts 5 years; contraceptive + therapeutic
Very effective; 3-5 year duration; user does not control use after insertion
Effective; 3-monthly or 1-monthly formulations; no user-dependent action after injection
Reversible; can cycle on/off; user-dependent; additional non-contraceptive benefits
Suitable for breastfeeding; safe postpartum; desogestrel form is highly effective

text: In low-resource settings with limited supply chains, injectable DMPA and copper IUDs are the most practical tier-1 contraceptive options due to their ease of administration, high effectiveness, and long duration. Combined oral contraceptives are second-line due to daily user-dependent adherence.
Emergency Contraception

caption: Emergency Contraception Options
rows: Within 72 hours of unprotected intercourse (best if <24 hours)
Over-the-counter in many countries; well-tolerated; no contraindications
Not widely available; more expensive; good option if presented 72-120 hours
>99% effective as emergency contraception and ongoing contraception
Provides long-term contraception; most effective emergency method; requires insertion by trained provider

text: Postpartum Care Essentials
Postpartum Pain Relief and Uterotonic Continuation

caption: Postpartum Pain Management and Uterine Involution Support
rows: If ibuprofen contraindicated; safe in breastfeeding
Uterotonic for PPH prevention (if not given in third stage)
If AMTSL not performed; reduce risk of late PPH
Contraindicated in hypertension; causes sustained uterine contraction

text: Essential Medicines for Neonatal Care
Neonatal Resuscitation and Initial Management

items: Epinephrine (1:10,000): 0.1-0.3 mL/kg IV or intraosseous for severe bradycardia or asystole during resuscitation. Keep readily available for neonatal emergency.
Suction equipment and resuscitation bags: Essential in every delivery room. Trained birth attendants must be skilled in neonatal resuscitation.
Vitamin K: 1 mg IM within first hour of birth to prevent hemorrhagic disease of the newborn. Critical if delivery outside hospital setting.
Eyedrops (tetracycline 1% or povidone-iodine 2.5%): For prevention of neonatal gonococcal ophthalmia within first hour of birth.
Chlorhexidine 7.1% solution: For cleaning the umbilical cord stump on day 0 and day 7 (in low-resource settings lacking access to antenatal care).

text: All these neonatal medicines and supplies must be available at every delivery point. Stock expiration dates must be monitored regularly, and staff must be trained in their proper use.
